title: Managed Agents
subtitle: Agents in Anthropic's cloud: sessions, sandbox, and life without your own server

theory.text: A third way to build agents

theory.md:
You already know two ways: hand-build the loop on the Messages API, or take the Agent SDK. Both share one trait — **the hosting is yours**: the agent runs on your server, and you provide it a sandbox for executing code, state storage, restarts, and scaling.

**Managed Agents** are a third path: the agent lives entirely **in Anthropic's cloud**. You describe the agent's configuration through the API and start sessions — while the agent loop, the isolated container for tools, history storage, and orchestration are handled by the platform. Your application just sends messages and reads a stream of events.

A handy way to remember the distinction is the question "who hosts?": the Messages API and Agent SDK give you the mechanism, but you host it; Managed Agents give you both the mechanism and the hosting.

The model is built around three concepts:

- **Agent** — a saved configuration: model, system prompt, set of tools. Created **once** (`POST /v1/agents`), it gets an id and is versioned: update the prompt and a new version appears, while old sessions keep running on theirs.
- **Session** — a specific run of an agent (`POST /v1/sessions`): it references an agent by id, has a status (`running`, `idle`, `terminated`), and stores a history of events. One configuration — as many parallel sessions as you like.
- **Sandbox (container)** — an isolated environment the platform spins up for each session: this is where the agent's tools run — bash, file operations, code. You can mount resources to a session: files or a git repository.

Communication happens through **events**: you send `user.message`, and out of the session flow `agent.message`, `agent.tool_use`, status changes. You can read them as a stream (SSE) — like streaming, but for an entire working session of the agent.

theory.title: Agent once — a session per run
theory.code:
# 1. Create the agent (once; save the id in config)
agent = client.beta.agents.create(
    name="Report Builder",
    model="claude-opus-4-8",
    system="You are an analyst. You build reports from data and save the result to files.",
    tools=[{"type": "agent_toolset_20260401"}],  # built-in set: bash, files, search
)

# 2. For each task — a new session referencing the agent
session = client.beta.sessions.create(
    agent=agent.id,
    environment_id=env_id,  # environment template for the container
)

# 3. Open the event stream and send the task
stream = client.beta.sessions.events.stream(session_id=session.id)
client.beta.sessions.events.send(
    session.id,
    events=[{"type": "user.message",
             "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Build a report from data.csv"}]}],
)
for event in stream:
    print(event.type)  # agent.tool_use, agent.message, session.status_idle ...

theory.text: What the platform does for you

theory.md:
The list of "free" concerns is impressive:

- **Sandbox.** Code execution by the agent is the scary part of self-hosted solutions: you need isolation, limits, cleanup. Here the container is spun up and disposed of by the platform.
- **State.** Session history is stored on the server: the client can drop, reconnect, and read the remaining events — no work is lost.
- **Long tasks.** A session lives independently of your process: the agent can work on a task while your laptop is closed.
- **Orchestration.** Queues, retries, parallel sessions, auto-compaction of an overflowing context — not your code.
- **Secrets.** Tokens for external services are kept in secure stores (vaults) and injected into requests outside the sandbox — the agent's code never sees them.

Plus observability: every session has a trace in the platform console — you can see every step the agent takes.

rows: Long autonomous tasks: reports, migrations, research
The session outlives your process; sandbox and state are the platform's job
You don't want to maintain infrastructure for code execution
Container isolation and lifecycle are the most expensive part of self-hosting
The agent must work with a developer's local files
A managed agent lives in the cloud and can't see the local machine
Strict requirements: your own perimeter, your own policies, full control
Messages API / Agent SDK on your own infrastructure
Hosting it yourself is the price of control

theory.bad: A weekly report service: your own worker on a VPS, a homemade Docker sandbox for the agent's code, your own task queue, your own history storage, your own retry mechanism — and all of it needs to be monitored and fixed.
theory.good: A weekly report service: a managed agent is created once with the needed prompt and tools; on a schedule a session is created, the app reads the event stream and picks up the finished result files.
theory.explanation: The substantive part (prompt, tools, report logic) is the same in both variants — the difference is the plumbing. The self-hosted variant drags in a sandbox, a queue, storage, and on-call duty; managed reduces it all to three API calls. Self-hosting is justified when control over the infrastructure is a requirement, not a habit.

theory.md: The Managed Agents overview (platform.claude.com/docs/en/managed-agents/overview) describes the architecture: an Agent is a saved, versioned configuration (model, system prompt, tools), a Session is a stateful run referencing an agent, and for each session the platform spins up an isolated container where the tools run. Interaction is through sending events and reading them as a stream (SSE); the order is strict: first the agent is created, then sessions reference it by id.

The main beginner anti-pattern is creating a new agent on every task run. An Agent is like a class, and a Session is like its instance: create the configuration once, save the id, and reuse it. When behavior changes — update the agent (a new version appears), don't spawn clones.

tasks.question: What is the main difference between Managed Agents and an agent on the Agent SDK?
options: Managed Agents run on different, more powerful models
The agent and the sandbox for its tools are hosted at Anthropic — no server of your own is needed for the agent
There's no difference — it's one technology with different names
tasks.explanation: The key is "who hosts." The Agent SDK is a library: the agent runs on your machine or server, and the sandbox is your concern. Managed Agents live in Anthropic's cloud: the platform holds the agent loop, the container for tools, session history, and orchestration, while your code only sends messages and reads events.
tasks.hint: Ask the question from the lesson: on whose server does the agent run?

tasks.question: How do Agent and Session relate in Managed Agents?
options: Agent is a saved configuration (created once), Session is a specific run of it; one agent can have many sessions
They're synonyms for the same object
A Session is created once, and an Agent — on every request
Agent is the user, Session is their password
tasks.explanation: An Agent relates to a Session as a class does to an instance: the configuration (model, prompt, tools) is created once, versioned, and reused, while a new session is created for each task referencing the agent id. Creating an agent on every run is the main anti-pattern: clones multiply and the point of versioning is lost.
tasks.hint: Recall the analogy from the lesson: class and instance.

tasks.instruction: The script runs on a schedule every hour — and after a week the console has hundreds of identical agents. Find the line with the architectural mistake.
tasks.explanation: `agents.create` is called inside the hourly job — each run creates a new clone agent. The right way: create the agent once (in a separate setup script), save its id in configuration, and in hourly_job only create sessions: `sessions.create(agent=SAVED_AGENT_ID, ...)`. The agent is a long-lived configuration, the session is a one-off run.

tasks.instruction: Assemble the correct lifecycle of working with a managed agent: configuration → run → stream → task → results. Discard the extra steps.
blocks: Create the agent once: model, system prompt, tools — save the agent id
For a new task, create a session referencing the agent id
Send a user.message event with the task statement
Read events until the idle status and pick up the results
distractors: Before each task, create a new agent with the same configuration
Deploy your own Docker container for executing the agent's code
tasks.explanation: The canonical loop: agent once → session per task → stream opened before sending (so you don't miss the first events) → message → reading until idle. The traps: cloning agents on every run is an anti-pattern, and your own container isn't needed — the platform spins up the sandbox, which is the whole point of the managed approach.

tasks.question: In which cases is self-hosting an agent preferable to Managed Agents? Select all that apply.
options: The agent must work with local files on a developer's machine
Strict placement requirements: everything must run in your own perimeter
You don't want to maintain sandbox and queue infrastructure
You need long autonomous tasks that outlive a client restart
tasks.explanation: Your own infrastructure is justified when the cloud fundamentally doesn't fit: the agent needs a local machine (that's Agent SDK territory) or policy requires working in your own perimeter. The options about "not wanting to maintain a sandbox" and "long autonomous tasks" are, on the contrary, the main arguments FOR managed: that's exactly what the platform takes on.
tasks.hint: Two of the four options are arguments for managed. Find the other two.
